data.title: Prudential: Stability, Value, And Cash Flow In One Package
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In an environment of more stable rates and margin pressure in the insurance sector, Prudential Financial (NYSE:PRU) is positioned as a defensive but value option. The company combines mature and resilient businesses with an expanding institutional asset management segment, supporting a strong and predictable return.

Q1 2025 confirmed the strength of PRU. Although net profit showed a decrease in special accounting effects, the core business performed very well. Adjusted operating income after tax grew 7% YoY ($1.19B), thanks to improvement in its key segments, especially in the US and group insurance.

In Individual Life, the company managed to reduce its operating losses. For its part, PGIM, the asset management division, maintained positive flows and increased assets under management despite lower revenue from performance commissions. In addition, PRU improved its liquidity position and maintained an attractive return for its shareholders, with a dividend yielding around 5.6%.

From a valuation point of view, PRU is traded with a P/E FWD of less than 8x, with a P/B just below 1x, and is supported by a model that balances stable earnings with upside via asset management. The stock does not promise exponential growth, but an attractive combination of operating profitability, capital efficiency and a consistent distribution policy.

In line with this, I consider it a good medium- to long-term buy option, especially for investors who prioritize cash flow, visibility and diversified exposure to the financial sector with low relative volatility.

Overview

In 2025, Prudential Financial is consolidated as one of the world's leading companies in the intersection between insurance, retirement solutions and institutional asset management.

Based in Newark and with more than 145 years of history, it combines its legacy with a clear commitment to modernize its business model. In an environment that requires capital efficiency, technology and customer focus, PRU is moving steadily forward on three main axes: growing its global asset management platform, evolving its offer in the US, and selectively expanding into markets such as Japan and Brazil.

PGIM, its investment arm, is a key driver of this strategy. With multiple specialized boutiques, it offers institutional access to real estate, fixed income, private equity, and infrastructure. In a market that migrates towards alternative assets -and where large mandates increasingly seek active management and depth-PGIM stands out for attracting sustained flows and for its direct exposure to structural issues such as energy transition or demography. More than a revenue generator, it is a strategic advantage.

In the US, PRU maintains leadership in group insurance, individual life and hybrid retirement products. Solutions like FlexGuard, which combine market exposure with some protection, reflect what many customers are looking for today: smooth performance. To this is added a strong investment in technology, which positions it as one of the most advanced insurers in process automation and digital experience. It is also adapting its offer to the new capital rules of the NAIC, redesigning its portfolio to sustain profitability with less regulatory requirements.

The international operation remains a strategic pillar. Japan, the world's second-largest insurance market, has an aging population with a high propensity to save, and PRU is well-positioned to capture this demand with long-term retirement products. At the same time, Brazil offers room for growth in a still underserved system, where digitalization and the formalization of savings point to a market with much to build.

Globally, the sector is growing at a steady pace. According to Swiss Re, premiums rise moderately, driven by the recovery in Asia and Latin America and increased awareness of financial planning. In the US alone, more than 11,000 people retire every day, which explains why retirement solutions are now a focal point for insurers like PRU.

Looking to the future, Prudential faces the same challenge as many of its peers: continue to grow in a world with stable rates, tighter margins, and competition from native digital models. But unlike others, PRU moves with discipline, without promising shortcuts. Its scale, reputation, and consistency continue to position it as one of the companies best prepared to build sustained value in a business where trust is more important than any other.

Q1 2025

In Q1 2025, PRU showed a solid operating performance, even against a lower net profit derived from extraordinary accounting factors. The company reported net attributable income of $707MM or $1.96 per share, down 38% YoY from $1.19B or $3.12 per share in the same period last year. This decline is mainly explained by losses on revaluation of profits with market risk and investment adjustments.

However, from an operational perspective, the results were positive: after-tax adjusted operating income reached $1.19B or $3.29 per share, representing a growth of 7% YoY, driven by better results in the company's core businesses.

The strength of PRU is also reflected in its balance sheet. Adjusted book value per share remained solid at $96.37, just below the previous year's $97.03. The GAAP book value rose to $83.59 from $75.00, implying an 11% improvement YoY. Assets under management (AUM) grew modestly to $1.52T (+2% YoY), benefiting from the recovery of financial markets and positive net flows.

In line with its shareholder return policy, Prudential returned $736MM in the quarter, including $486MM in dividends (equivalent to a dividend yield of 5.6% on adjusted book value) and $250MM in share buybacks. The holding's liquidity position also improved to $4.9B from $4.2B a year ago.

As for the operating segments, PGIM, the global asset management division, reported adjusted operating income of $156MM, a decline of 8% YoY, due to lower revenues from co-investments and performance fees.

However, PGIM's AUM grew 3% YoY to $1.39T, thanks to positive net flows of $4.3B, especially from institutional clients in fixed income, private equity, and equities. This demonstrates the structural resilience and institutional relevance of PGIM, despite a weaker quarter in alternative revenues.

U.S. business showed an operating growth of 16% YoY, reaching $931MM. In that segment, Retirement Strategies remained stable at $876MM, with Institutional Retirement falling to $412MM (-7% YoY) and Individual Retirement rising to $464MM (+5% YoY), driven by the sale of indexed products. Individual Retirement sales totaled $3.5B (+5% YoY).

At Group Insurance, operating income doubled to $89MM (+98% YoY), thanks to a significant improvement in life and disability outcomes. For its part, Individual Life reduced its operating loss to $34MM from $121MM since Q1 2024, with sales growing 26% YoY to $210MM.

The international segment posted operating revenues of $848MM, implying a 5% year-on-year decline, affected by lower investment margins, lower profits from joint ventures, and an adverse currency impact. Despite this, sales in constant currency grew 15% YoY to $586MM, with good performance in both Japan and Brazil, confirming the strategic value of the international business, although subject to external swings.

The corporate segment and others showed a loss of $415MM, which improved slightly compared to $435MM a year ago, thanks to a cut in expenses partially offset by a higher financial burden. The total of negative off-trade accounting adjustments reached $600MM before tax, including $351MM for change in market profits, $246MM in investment losses, and $73MM in divested or run-off businesses.

Valuation

PRU is quoted at a clear discount, although this is due to a weak growth profile and structurally low profitability. The P/E is 8.17x TTMy 7.88x FWD, against a sector average of 11.8x and 10.9x, implying a discount of 30% and 28% respectively. This compression on the multiple occurs even when EPS growth is expected to be 7.68% in 2025 and 8.92% in 2026. However, subsequent projections show a slowdown: 3.05% in 2027 and a fall of -2.87% in 2028.

The valuation is also contained in metrics on revenues: the EV/Sales is 1.19x TTM and 1.31x FWD, when the sector is traded at 3.19x and 3.08x, respectively. The Price/Sales is even lower, at 0.63x TTM and 0.69x FWD, with a discount of more than 70% compared to the sector in both readings. This confirms that the market is punishing PRU not only for its low profit growth, but also for the perceived quality of its revenues.

On the growth side, the data is consistent with this caution. Revenue drops -0.13% YoY and is only expected to improve 4.09% FWD when the sector projects 6.39%. EBITDA dropped by 16.5% YoY and EBIT by 9.33%, although an EBIT recovery of 8.61% is expected in the next 12 months.

In terms of EPS, the adjusted data show a growth of 7.49% YoY and 8.40% FWD, although the structural growth projected for 3-5 years is just 7.66%, below the 9.54% sectoral.

Operating cash flow drops dramatically (-92.54% YoY), and although there is a rebound projection of 10.39% FWD, the reading is clear: cash generation is very weak. This is also reflected in a Price/Cash Flow TTM of 48.94x, well above the industry average (9.48x). The ROE figures grew 12.09% in the last year and are projected to have a 5.77% FWD, which is remarkable, but starts from a low base: the total ROE is only 7.95%, compared to 10.22% of the sector.

Overall profitability is weak. The gross margin is just 25.79%, while the sector stands at 60.63%. The EBIT margin is 4.54%, compared to 22.84% sectoral. The net margin accompanies this weakness, with just 3.80% against 22.50%. The return on assets (ROA) is minimal: 0.31% against 1.12% for the sector.

Even with a strong cash position ($38.06B) and an attractive dividend of 4.95%, leverage remains high, with a total debt of $47.71B and an EV of $71.91B. This financial structure contributes to the fact that the multiples EV/EBITDA and EV/EBIT are extremely high: 25.49x and 26.25x TTM, although an improvement is projected with an EV/EBIT FWD of 8.11x.

Risks

Investing in PRU involves taking risks that go beyond numbers. As a financial and insurance company, your business is closely linked to the business cycle. In contexts of slowdown or volatility, such as the current one, PRU may face declines in revenues, pressure on margins, and increased regulatory demands.

The sensitivity to interest rates, inflation, and credit risk is structural in character, since a change in any of these variables can alter not only its profitability but also its balance sheet.

In addition, the sector is currently undergoing a profound transformation. Traditional models such as PRU increasingly compete with digital platforms, insurtechs, and lighter structures that operate at lower costs and with more flexibility. The scale that used to be an advantage can now become a brake if there is no real capacity for adaptation.

PRU also faces the challenge of managing long-term assets in uncertain markets. Its future commitments require a precise and conservative capital allocation, although it does not always have room to avoid exposure to market risk. Also, US regulations and globally are becoming increasingly demanding, which could affect their operations or raise compliance costs.

Finally, because these are very sensitive products — life insurance, pensions, long-term savings — any legal dispute, operational error, or reputational problem can have a very strong impact. It is for this reason that trust is a key asset for PRU, and to sustain it in a competitive and unstable environment is a risk in itself.

Conclusions

I consider PRU a solid and quite attractive investment today, trading with a P/E FWD below 8x and a P/B close to 1x. It is not an action for those who seek spectacular growth, but for those who value constant profitability, good capital management, and a clear dividend policy.

Therefore, I think it is an opportunity to buy with a medium to long-term approach. PRU doesn't have to reinvent itself; its true strength lies in consistency and prudence, which makes it a reliable bet in a market where stability is increasingly valuable.

data.title: T. Rowe Price: The S&P 500 V-Shape Recovery Is A Tailwind To Its Business (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
T. Rowe Price (NASDAQ:TROW) is an American asset management firm with $1.57 trillion in assets under management and is part of the exclusive list of dividend aristocrats, having increased its dividend for 39 consecutive years. This is my third time covering TROW, and in my latest rating in February, I considered it the right time to sell the shares due to their Q4 performance, during which the company displayed weakness despite favorable market conditions following Donald Trump's election.

Today, I see the company's outlook a bit brighter, and in this analysis, I will explain my reasons why the stock is no longer a "sell" and what situations have improved, allowing me to consider the stock a "hold."

T. Rowe Price Backdrop

in USD Q1 2025 Q1 2024 Q1 2023
Net flows -8.6B -8B -16.1B
Assets Under Management 1.57T 1.54T 1.34T

Source: Stock Analysis

First, although the assets under management of T. Rowe Price have increased since 2023 due to positive market performance, net flows have been negative during the past Q1s, indicating that investors are withdrawing more funds than they are depositing through T. Rowe Price's products. Ultimately, inflows are what drive the sustainability of the AUM, and this trend has been a sign of concern for T. Rowe Price investors. Nonetheless, the aspect that calms investors is that these cumulative outflows of $32 billion over the past three Q1s represent only a slight two percent of the company's AUM.

In million USD Q1 2025 Q1 2024 Q1 2023
Revenue 1,764 1,750 1,538
Revenue growth 0.8% 13.8%

Source: Seeking Alpha

Furthermore, negative outflows haven't represented negative revenue growth, as the effect of higher asset levels and AUM has led to a rise in management fees collected.

These negative outflows are being driven by secular trends in investors' preferences, which are transitioning away from open-ended mutual funds as a vehicle and active equity as an asset class. This has affected TROW, as a large portion of its AUM is in this format. As a result, management still expects negative outflows to continue in FY 2025. Yet, management has high expectations for flows within Fixed Income and Alternative Assets.

At the same time, to address the long-term headwinds, the company has begun launching products in an ETF format. For example, they have recently launched two transparent equity ETFs: Hedged Equity (THEQ) and Capital Appreciation Income (TCAL), bringing the family's total to 19 ETFs. The problem here is that the ETF business only has $12.5 billion in AUM, which is minor compared to the $773.4 billion in Equity AUM achieved in Q1, and therefore is unlikely to be a significant revenue contributor in the short term.

Stock Market Recovery & Financial Characteristics

The main reason for changing my previous "sell" rating to a "hold" is due to TROW's commission structure. Since February, with the stock market turmoil, the stock of T. Rowe Price suffered, as this is an equity-heavy asset manager, and lower asset levels inherently mean lower management fees for T. Rowe Price. Nonetheless, TROW has the advantage that most of its products are charged daily, which is a tailwind based on the V-shaped recovery of the S&P 500, with a modest drawdown duration of approximately four months. At the same time, in Q2, the market experienced a rapid rebound following the announcement of reciprocal tariffs, and since May, the index has been reaching quarterly highs.

Although T. Rowe Price is a consolidated asset manager with modest growth, they have appealing characteristics for income-oriented investors. First, this is a company with a strong balance sheet, totaling $2.837 billion in cash, representing 12.7% of its market capitalization, with no long-term debt. At the same time, they have a high dividend yield of 5.02%, which has had an appealing 10-year CAGR of 10.09%. Nonetheless, given the current outlook, it is unlikely that this dividend will rise at the same rate on a forward-looking basis, due to the challenges posed by active equity mutual funds. Simultaneously, the excess share repurchases over share issuance add a buyback yield of 0.73%.

Valuation
